rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we saw 150 ADCs didn't make it in the clinic, predominantly solid tumor targets, and they fall in this bucket of 50,000 or low copies"
Speaker said: "we improved the safety as well, no findings at one mg or three mg per kg, but reversible reticulocyte decrease at nine mg per kg"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Conventional monospecific ADCs targeting low‑copy solid‑tumor antigens
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Heterogeneous low antigen copy number limits internalization and therapeutic window

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that the format required extensive engineering to achieve manufacturability and monomer purity, indicating a narrow set of viable formats
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Yield and monomer percentage comparable only to Trastuzumab, suggesting limited scalability
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on dual‑target expression may limit applicability to tumors lacking co‑expressed antigens

key_data_gaps: No in‑human efficacy or safety data presented
Lack of pharmacodynamic biomarker linking target engagement to response

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for quantitative biomarkers to identify patients with sufficient co‑expression of the two antigens (e.g., MUC16 and NaPi2b) to benefit from MUDA ADCs
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we looked at the target expansion in these ovarian patients, looking at the RNA data at this point, and it is 90% coverage actually"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate multi‑omic expression data to predict dual‑antigen coverage and prioritize patients for MUDA ADC trials

crispro_opportunity.description: Explore synergistic combinations with immune‑modulating agents to overcome residual heterogeneity after MUDA ADC treatment
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that simple combination of two monospecific ADCs did not improve efficacy, suggesting need for novel partners
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model pathway dependencies post‑ADC exposure to suggest rational combination candidates

cited_competitors.name: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu)
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as benchmark ADC with toxicity limitations

unresolved_questions: Will the MUDA platform retain efficacy in tumors with dynamic antigen loss after treatment?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to balance efficacy and the observed reversible reticulocyte decrease at high dose?

talk_metadata.talk_title: High‑throughput drug screening & single‑cell network analysis in IDH mutant glioma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we identified three distinct vulnerability programs, each actionable with existing compounds"
Speaker said: "current standard of care achieves disease stabilization but is not curative"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Cell‑state heterogeneity driven by master regulator programs (e.g., Hes4‑Notch, EGFR‑Neuroligin‑1) that are not targeted by IDH inhibition alone
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "there's eventually progression because this heterogeneity is not addressed"

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that IDH inhibition stabilizes disease, yet concluded that "current therapy is not curative" without presenting data on long‑term survival

key_data_gaps: No in‑vivo validation of predicted drug‑state matches yet
Absence of overall survival or progression‑free survival endpoints

crispro_opportunity.description: Target master regulator‑driven cell states (e.g., DNA‑damage repair in AC‑like, MAPK in NPC‑like) with existing drugs identified by OncoTreat
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "In the AC‑like state, cisplatin and enibritib both target DNA damage and repair as a key weakness"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can prioritize MR‑driven vulnerabilities across patients and suggest optimal drug‑state pairings

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate single‑cell MR profiling as a biomarker endpoint in upcoming IDH inhibitor trials
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described a pipeline turning MR programs into drug predictions
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can generate MR‑based stratification schemas for adaptive trial arms

cited_competitors.name: IDH inhibitors (generic)
cited_competitors.context: standard of care for IDH mutant glioma

unresolved_questions: Will the identified state‑specific drugs achieve durable responses in vivo?
How stable are the master regulator programs over treatment cycles?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Chinese research team (collaboration with McMaster, etc.)
talk_metadata.talk_title: Serum hyaluronic acid as predictive biomarker of T‑DXD‑induced interstitial lung disease
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "T‑DXD induced ILD rate is about 15% across all tumor types, fatal event rate about 2.2%"
Speaker said: "In young mice ILD rate is 0% compared with 40% in adult mice"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T‑DXD) – ADC‑induced interstitial lung disease
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Age‑dependent accumulation in alveolar macrophages leading to HYAL2‑mediated hyaluronic acid release
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker said: "we see reversible reticulocyte decrease at nine mg per kg" (referring to ADC safety, indicating dose‑dependent toxicity concerns)

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Retrospective pediatric T‑DXD study (no NCT provided)
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No age‑stratified safety monitoring; pediatric patients not prospectively screened for lung vulnerability
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Serum hyaluronic acid or HA‑panel not incorporated at enrollment
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Small sample size (~30 patients) limits confidence in AUC estimates

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted severe ILD risk and age‑dependent lung accumulation, suggesting a safety liability in the platform
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad use across indications without robust predictive biomarker for ILD

key_data_gaps: No prospective validation of HA biomarker in larger adult cohort
Lack of mechanistic human tissue data (e.g., BAL or lung biopsies)

crispro_opportunity.description: Implement serum HA (alone or in panel) as a prospective companion diagnostic to exclude high‑risk patients before T‑DXD initiation
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker reported AUC of 74% for HA alone and 93% when combined with other fibrosis markers
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate longitudinal HA kinetics with patient covariates to generate risk scores for ILD

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine T‑DXD with IVIG or other macrophage‑modulating agents to mitigate AM‑driven toxicity
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker mentioned successful IVIG rescue in a grade‑4 ILD case
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model immune‑cell interaction networks to predict synergistic protective combos

cited_competitors.name: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu)
cited_competitors.context: benchmark ADC with ILD safety signal

unresolved_questions: Will HA biomarker retain predictive performance in a prospective, multi‑center adult trial?
What is the mechanistic link between HYAL2 induction and clinical ILD severity?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Co‑clinical trial of datopotamab deruxtecan (Dato) in patient‑derived xenografts
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Overall, Dato significantly extended time to tumor doubling in 13 of the 14 pretreatment models"
Speaker said: "Combination of Dato and gemcitabine was highly effective at controlling tumor growth"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Dato monotherapy in Trop‑2 low‑expressing tumors
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Reliance on Trop‑2 expression; two progressive‑disease models were Trop‑2 negative yet showed limited response
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted that even Trop‑2 negative models sometimes extended time to tumor doubling, suggesting partial off‑target activity

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Biopsies taken only pre‑treatment; lack of prospective biomarker stratification for Trop‑2 levels
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Quantitative Trop‑2 expression threshold for enrollment
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Biopsy take rate ~47% limits representativeness of cohort

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Efficacy appears tightly linked to Trop‑2 expression; platform may be vulnerable to antigen loss or heterogeneity
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad basket design without molecular selection may dilute signal

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented robust PDX‑patient concordance (78.6%) yet admitted that 22% of pairs were discordant, highlighting a gap between model fidelity and clinical prediction

key_data_gaps: Limited number of tumor types with low Trop‑2 expression

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a quantitative Trop‑2 expression cutoff using CrisPRO to select patients most likely to benefit from Dato
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted variability in Trop‑2 levels across the 20 PDX models
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate IHC, RNA‑seq and proteomics to generate a composite Trop‑2 score for trial enrollment

crispro_opportunity.description: Prioritize gemcitabine as a rational combination for Trop‑2‑positive tumors based on PDX synergy data
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker demonstrated that Dato + gemcitabine markedly reduced tumor volume in pancreatic and bladder PDXs
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict synergistic DNA‑damage response interactions for ADC payloads

cited_competitors.name: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu)
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as another ADC with established clinical activity

unresolved_questions: What molecular features beyond Trop‑2 predict the observed partial responses?
Can real‑time PDX generation be streamlined to guide treatment decisions within a clinically actionable timeframe?

rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The interaction was synergistic specifically in TP53 mutant and knockout cells"
Speaker said: "Adding a WEE1 inhibitor after the priming increased cell death from 30% to nearly 80%"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: PARP inhibitor monotherapy in TP53‑mutant tumors
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: TP53 loss leads to reliance on base excision repair; PARP inhibition alone does not induce lethal DNA damage without additional replication stress
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted that PARP inhibitor alone showed limited efficacy despite high DNA damage markers

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: First‑in‑human trial of the inducer‑amplifier regimen (no NCT provided)
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Base excision repair inhibition (TAS‑102) + PARP inhibition + WEE1 inhibition
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No stratification by TP53 mutation status reported in early trial design
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: TP53 mutation confirmation and BER activity assay
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Early safety data only; efficacy endpoints not yet powered

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Inducer‑amplifier three‑drug regimen (TAS‑102 + PARP inhibitor + WEE1 inhibitor)
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Relies on sequential dosing schedule; any deviation may reduce synergy, indicating a procedural vulnerability
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Complex three‑drug schedule may limit patient adherence and increase drug‑drug interaction risk

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that PARP inhibition alone increased DNA damage but did not cause cell death, yet concluded that adding WEE1 inhibitor converts damage to lethal mitotic catastrophe

key_data_gaps: Unclear toxicity profile of three‑drug combination in humans

crispro_opportunity.description: Reposition existing PARP inhibitors for TP53‑mutant cancers using the inducer‑amplifier framework
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker demonstrated preclinical synergy of PARP inhibition with TAS‑102 and WEE1 blockade
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synthetic lethal networks to identify optimal dosing sequences for existing PARP inhibitors

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate TP53 mutation testing and BER activity biomarkers as eligibility criteria
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that mutant cells retain EDU longer, indicating a measurable biomarker
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can define quantitative thresholds for BER activity to enrich for responders

cited_competitors.name: Mosaic Therapeutics MDM2‑PARP inhibitor combo
cited_competitors.context: questioned during Q&A about wild‑type TP53 activation

unresolved_questions: What is the maximum tolerated dose of the three‑drug regimen in TP53‑mutant patients?
Will the sequential schedule be feasible in community oncology settings?

assets_to_track: PARP inhibitors under investigation for TP53‑mutant cancers

talk_metadata.talk_title: KBD‑1111: a selective, long‑acting, brain‑penetrant PARP‑1 inhibitor
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "KBD‑1111 shows 9,000‑fold selectivity for PARP‑1 over PARP‑2"
Speaker said: "In rats, QW dosing showed no significant reticulocyte decrease, unlike QD dosing"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: PARP‑2 inhibition drives hematologic toxicity (anemia, thrombocytopenia)
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted that olaparib caused significant reticulocyte reduction while KBD‑1111 QW regimen did not

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Phase 1 first‑in‑human trial of KBD‑1111 (no NCT provided)
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Selective PARP‑1 inhibition with brain penetration
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No explicit CNS‑metastasis stratification mentioned; early trial may enroll heterogeneous HRD patients
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: PARP‑1 activity or HRD status to enrich for responders
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Early safety cohort; efficacy not yet powered

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that selectivity is based on a 9,000‑fold IC50 gap, but structural details were not revealed, suggesting limited IP breadth
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Focus on Q2W dosing may limit flexibility for combination regimens requiring daily dosing

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented superior in‑vitro trapping data yet could not provide PD data to explain the claimed Q2W dosing advantage

key_data_gaps: No human efficacy or CNS activity data yet
Unclear long‑term safety of brain penetration

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage KBD‑1111’s brain penetration to target PARP‑dependent DNA repair in CNS metastases where first‑gen PARP inhibitors fail
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted CSF AUC comparable to AZD9574 and superior brain penetration (Kpu 0.26)
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model CNS drug distribution and predict patient subsets with brain metastases likely to benefit

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a PARP‑1 activity assay to select HRD‑positive patients for KBD‑1111 monotherapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized selectivity and improved therapeutic index
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate genomic HRD scores with pharmacodynamic markers to define a responder cohort

cited_competitors.name: Olaparib
cited_competitors.context: first‑generation PARP inhibitor used as safety comparator

cited_competitors.name: AZD5305
cited_competitors.context: next‑generation selective PARP‑1 inhibitor used for potency comparison

unresolved_questions: Will Q2W dosing maintain sufficient PARP‑1 inhibition in combination with DNA‑damaging chemotherapy?
What is the incidence of CNS‑related adverse events at therapeutic exposures?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Comparative activity of TEAD central‑pocket inhibitor vs. YAP‑TEAD PPI in KRAS‑mutant cancers
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The combination of the Yap/TED PPI with sotorasib gave a much more durable and pronounced extended response"
Speaker said: "Both inhibitors were able to provide similar therapeutic benefit in vivo"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: KRAS G12C monotherapy (sotorasib) in resistant cell lines
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Hippo pathway activation (YAP/TAZ‑TEAD transcription) bypasses KRAS blockade
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "cells became resistant after a period of around two weeks of dosing"

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted a rapid rebound of TEAD transcriptional activity within 24 h, suggesting limited duration of target engagement
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Potential need for continuous dosing to maintain suppression, which may affect tolerability

cognitive_dissonance: In vitro data showed superior durability of the PPI versus the CPI, yet in vivo xenograft models both modalities yielded comparable tumor regression

key_data_gaps: Lack of pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic correlation for the rebound effect
No clinical trial data yet for the combination

crispro_opportunity.description: Pair KRAS G12C inhibitors with TEAD PPI (IAG 933) in KRAS‑mutant NSCLC to overcome Hippo‑mediated resistance
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker demonstrated durable in‑vitro response of the PPI + sotorasib combination
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate adaptive resistance trajectories and predict optimal dosing schedules for sustained TEAD suppression

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate early TEAD‑target gene (CTGF) rebound monitoring as a pharmacodynamic biomarker in KRAS‑TEAD combination trials
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker observed a 24‑hour rebound of TEAD transcriptional activity with the PPI
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model time‑course PD to define sampling windows for biomarker readouts

cited_competitors.name: AZD0751 (central‑pocket TEAD inhibitor)
cited_competitors.context: compared directly with IAG 933 in vitro and in vivo

cited_competitors.name: Sotorasib (KRAS G12C inhibitor)
cited_competitors.context: standard KRAS inhibitor used as backbone for combination

unresolved_questions: Why does the in‑vitro superiority of the PPI not translate to enhanced in‑vivo efficacy?
What dosing regimen can prevent the 24‑h TEAD transcriptional rebound observed with IAG 933?
